ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fish cake entry (FOODON_03315595); fish tofu is a specific variant incorporating tofu and surimi — mapped as closest_parent. No exact FoodOn URI for fish tofu exists as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fish cake broadly; fish tofu with surimi and tofu is a specific processed variant. Used as closest_parent defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish tofu or fish cake as a food product in the clinical terminology as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fish tofu is approximately 3 pieces (about 90g), commonly served as a hotpot ingredient, skewer snack, or side dish in Singapore hawker and restaurant settings.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Tofu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fish Tofu (cr_id F03040007). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this primary source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Fish Cake and Processed Fish Products — analogous estimate based on surimi-based fish cake GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fish tofu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low category) based on analogous surimi-based fish cake products reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. The product contains 12.1g carbohydrate per 100g, primarily from starch and tofu; the protein and fat content moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 90g serving = (40 × 12.1 × 0.90) / 100 = 4.4. The input record cited GI 15 from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; this estimate of 40 from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous data is used instead as the defensible approved-source value.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 524mg per 100g — exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for processed foods; manufactured fish paste and added salt are primary sodium drivers.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit fish tofu portion size to no more than 60g per meal, avoid pairing with soy sauce or salty dipping sauces, and choose steamed or soup-based preparations over deep-fried to minimise additional sodium and fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 12.1g/100g with sugar 4.8g/100g and only 1.2g dietary fibre; estimated GI of 40 (low) produces a moderate glycaemic load of 4.4 per 90g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume fish tofu in controlled portions (up to 90g), preferring steamed or soup-based preparations over deep-fried, avoiding sweet dipping sauces, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables to further blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (524mg/100g) combined with saturated fat (~2.5g/100g) and potential trans fat from deep-frying elevates cardiovascular risk; processed surimi contributes to overall sodium load.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fish tofu to small portions (≤60g), select steamed or boiled variants to avoid additional frying fats, avoid high-sodium condiments, and account for the sodium contribution within a daily limit of 2000mg sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; high sodium (524mg/100g) worsens tissue swelling; processing negates the anti-inflammatory benefit of tofu.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried fish tofu and choose steamed or soup-based variants, strictly limit portion size to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling, and be aware that processing and frying negate the inherent anti-inflammatory properties of tofu.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish tofu (鱼豆腐; Tauhu Ikan) is a popular processed fishcake product widely consumed in Singapore and across East Asia. It is made from a blended mixture of fish meat (typically white fish), tofu, and surimi (processed fish paste), combined with starch, egg white, salt, and seasonings, then shaped into rectangular or square blocks and either steamed or deep-fried. The texture is soft, slightly springy, and mildly savoury, with a subtle fish flavour balanced by the mild creaminess of tofu. Fish tofu is a common ingredient in hotpot (steamboat), skewer snacks, oden, and hawker-style dishes across Singapore, Malaysia, and East Asia. Per 100g, it provides 171 kcal, 8.3g protein, 10.0g fat, 12.1g carbohydrate, 4.8g sugar, 1.2g dietary fibre, 524mg sodium, 67mg calcium, and 0.2mg iron. The high sodium content (524mg/100g) makes it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The moderate carbohydrate content with low fibre is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management, particularly when deep-fried. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 40 (low category), yielding a glycaemic load of about 4.4 per 90g serving. Steamed or soup-based preparations are clinically preferable to deep-fried variants for all health-sensitive populations. Fish tofu contains fish, soy, and egg allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish tofu?
Is fish tofu suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat fish tofu if I have high blood pressure?
What is fish tofu made of?
Is fish tofu the same as fishcake?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040007) with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI source) as no direct measurement exists for fish tofu; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong nutrient provenance.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 2.5g/100g based on typical fat composition of processed fish and tofu products (HPB database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry); this is an approximation and should be updated when disaggregated fat data becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for fish tofu are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F03040007 as of April 2026; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this Singapore-specific processed product. 3. GI value of 15 from the input record's 'Embedded GI Database' was not used as this is not an approved source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI estimated at 40 (low) from analogous surimi-based fish cake data in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input record's dietary_tags listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' — these are incorrect as fish tofu contains fish meat and surimi; corrected to is_vegetarian: false and is_vegan: false. 5. Halal status set to true as fish tofu is typically manufactured to halal standards in Singapore; this should be verified against specific product certification for packaged variants.
